attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for a follow-up review of urinalysis performed two weeks ago due to intermittent lower urinary tract symptoms. The urinalysis showed 2+ blood and trace protein. He has no flank pain, and no history of recent illness. His medical history includes hypertension diagnosed five years ago. His only medication is perindopril. He works as a painter and has a 30-pack-year smoking history, having quit three years ago.

On examination, he appears well with a pulse rate of 76/min, blood pressure 138/88 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness.

Repeat urinalysis confirms microscopic haematuria (3+ blood) and trace protein. Urine microscopy shows 25-50 red blood cells per high-power field with no red cell casts. Serum creatinine is 85 μmol/L (60-110).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This 52-year-old man presents with persistent asymptomatic microscopic haematuria and trace proteinuria discovered during an insurance medical examination. Given his age, smoking history, and occupational exposure to chemicals as a painter, investigation to exclude urinary tract malignancy is warranted.

CT urography and cystoscopy is the most appropriate next step in management. This patient has multiple risk factors for urinary tract malignancy including age over 40, significant smoking history, and occupational chemical exposure. He requires investigation of the upper urinary tract via imaging and lower urinary tract with cystoscopy to exclude malignancy. CT urography is the gold standard for assessment of the urinary tract for malignancy. In a patient less than 50, renal tract ultrasonography is preferred as it removes risk of contrast-induced nephropathy and does not involve added radiation exposure.

Repeat urinalysis in six months would be inappropriate given the patient's risk factors and the persistence of haematuria on repeat testing. Delaying investigation could potentially miss a urinary tract malignancy at a treatable stage.

24-hour urine protein collection might be useful to quantify proteinuria, but it would not address the primary concern of excluding malignancy. The trace proteinuria in this case is less concerning than the persistent haematuria, especially given the patient's risk factors.

Empiric antibiotic therapy is not indicated as the patient has no urinary symptoms, no leukocytes in the urine, and persistent haematuria on repeat testing. Treating for a presumed infection would delay appropriate investigation.

Renal biopsy would be considered if glomerular disease was strongly suspected, typically with significant proteinuria, red cell casts, or impaired renal function. This patient has only trace proteinuria, normal renal function, and no red cell casts, making glomerular disease less likely than urological causes of haematuria.

After excluding malignancy with imaging and cystoscopy, if haematuria persists, further evaluation for non-malignant causes such as nephrolithiasis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, or glomerular disease may be warranted. The presence of even trace proteinuria alongside haematuria increases the likelihood of renal parenchymal disease, which may require nephrology consultation if urological causes are excluded.
learningPoints: Persistent microscopic haematuria in adults requires investigation to exclude urinary tract malignancy, especially in those over 40 years of age.
Urinary tract malignancy risk factors include smoking, occupational chemical exposure, and age over 40 years.
Renal causes of haematuria should be considered when proteinuria and/or hypertension are present alongside haematuria.
references: O'Connor E, McVey A, Demkiw S, Lawrentschuk N, Murphy DG. Assessment and management of haematuria in the general practice setting. Aust J Gen Pract. 2021 Jul;50(7):467-471. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-03-21-5892. PMID: 34189542.
Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) Management in Primary Care (5th edition). Kidney Health Australia. 2024. Available from: https://assets.kidney.org.au/resources/KHA-CKD-Handbook-5th-Ed-July2024.pdf
